data.title: Ukraine’s naval drones strike Russian oil tankers in Black Sea off Turkish coast
data.description:
Ukraine used domestically produced Sea Baby naval drones to strike two Russian oil tankers in the Black Sea, an official from Ukraine’s security services said on Saturday.
The two oil tankers, Kairos and Virat – said to be part of Russia’s “shadow fleet” that evade sanctions – were struck in quick succession off Turkey’s Black Sea coast late Friday afternoon.
The strikes prompted rescue operations by the Turkish coastguard and other teams. Crew members on board both vessels were reported to be safe.
The SBU official, who spoke on condition of anonymity because of the sensitivity of the operations, provided a video of the alleged attack, purporting to show the destruction of two tankers at sea.
The official said the two tankers were vessels that were sanctioned by the West, adding that Ukrainian intelligence continued “to take active steps to curtail Russia’s financial capabilities to wage war against Ukraine”.
Sea Baby naval drones had disabled vessels capable of transporting oil worth almost US$70 million, hindering Russia’s efforts to circumvent international sanctions, the official said.

Earlier, Turkey’s Transport and Infrastructure Minister Abdulkadir Uraloglu said the tankers were possibly struck by mines, missiles, a marine vessel, or a drone.
The incidents took place within Turkey’s exclusive economic zone, he said, noting that Turkish authorities were in contact with their counterparts to prevent a recurrence and to ensure navigational safety. He did not provide further details.
Uraloglu said the Gambian-flagged Kairos had caught fire, which was continuing in the vessel’s enclosed areas on Saturday. All 25 crew were safely evacuated.
The Virat, however, was not ablaze and no evacuation request was made, he added. Authorities had earlier reported a fire in the Virat’s engine room.
The captain of the Virat tanker had issued a distress call over “a drone attack”.
The OpenSanctions database, which tracks people or organisations involved in sanctions evasion, describes the vessels as part of a shadow fleet of ships used to evade sanctions imposed on Russia following its 2022 invasion of Ukraine.
Ukraine has carried out successful naval strikes against Russian shipping during the war, particularly using explosives-packed marine drones. However, Ukrainian missions have largely been limited to the waters of the northern Black Sea.
Officials said the Gambian-flagged Kairos caught fire in the Black Sea around 28 nautical miles (52km) off the coast of Turkey’s Kocaeli province. It was sailing empty from Egypt towards Russia’s Novorossiysk port.
Shortly after, the maritime authority reported that a second tanker, Virat, was “struck” while sailing in the Black Sea about 35 nautical miles (64km) off the Turkish coast.

The VesselFinder website showed the Virat was anchored north of the Bosphorus, not far from its current position, on November 4. The Kairos’ last position was on November 26 south of the Dardanelles Strait connecting the Aegean Sea and the Sea of Marmara.
The United States sanctioned the Virat in January this year, followed by the European Union, Switzerland, the United Kingdom and Canada, according to the OpenSanctions website. Similarly, the EU sanctioned the Kairos in July this year, followed by the UK and Switzerland.
“The shadow tanker fleet continues to provide multibillion-dollar revenues for the Kremlin bypassing sanctions, disguising its activities under the flags of third countries, using complex schemes to conceal owners and poses significant environmental threats,” OpenSanctions says in its website entry on the Kairos.
The Virat, built in 2018, uses “irregular and high-risk shipping practices” and has previously sailed under the flags of Barbados, Comoros, Liberia and Panama, OpenSanctions says.
The Kairos, formerly flagged as Panamanian, Greek and Liberian, was built in 2002.

data.title: Hong Kong begins 3 days of mourning as more arrests made over deadly blaze
data.description:
Hong Kong began a three-day official mourning period on Saturday for victims of the city’s deadliest blaze in decades as residents queued at the site of the tragedy and various halls to pay their respects, penning tributes and laying flowers.
For the first time since the fire broke out on Wednesday at Wang Fuk Court in Tai Po, the death toll remained at 128 on Saturday. No further human remains were recovered, while about 150 people remained “unreachable”, according to police.
Graft-busters arrested three people who had previously been detained for manslaughter but later released, this time for alleged corruption behind the renovation work at the estate, and national security police took away a university student for questioning after his posts online and distribution of fliers in public demanding an independent inquiry.
The day started on a sombre note under cloudy skies as Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu and principal officials gathered at government headquarters at 8am to observe a three-minute silence in honour of the victims of the inferno.

Liaison office chief Zhou Ji and two officials from Beijing’s Hong Kong and Macau Affairs Office (HKMAO), Xu Qifang and Nong Rong, who are in the city to help with disaster relief efforts, were among those present, all dressed in black.
Cui Jianchun, commissioner of the foreign ministry’s arm in Hong Kong, along with his deputies and staff, also observed three minutes of silence outside the office building, while soldiers from the local garrison of the People’s Liberation Army expressed condolences in ceremonies.
During the mourning period until Monday, national and Hong Kong flags at all government buildings and facilities will fly at half-mast.
Residents streamed into condolence halls set up in all 18 districts by authorities and wrote their tributes and laid flowers, adding to growing piles of bouquets as the day wore on.


But it was near ground zero in Tai Po where residents kept showing up, patiently queuing to leave flowers at a makeshift display site in the Kwong Fuk Sitting-out Area. In the backdrop stood the seven fire-ravaged blocks that had been ablaze for 43 hours before the flames were tamed on Friday morning.
Human resources director Marco Mok, 35, was among hundreds in the queue. He said he hoped that paying his respects would help bring some peace to the victims and their loved ones.
“I hope that the government and other organisations can handle whatever follows this episode well – no matter if it’s the families of those who died or those affected, they can be given good follow-up measures,” he said.
The HKMAO said that relevant parties had swiftly mobilised necessary supplies, following a request from the Hong Kong government, with the first batch of resources requested at the second stage delivered on Saturday night.

According to Beijing’s Ministry of Emergency Management, offices and foundations – including the Alibaba Foundation – had offered emergency supplies for Hong Kong, including portable outdoor charging stations, respirators and goggles.
The central government also urged mainland Chinese insurance companies and banks in Hong Kong to provide help and flexibility to the victims.
On the investigation front, the Independent Commission Against Corruption arrested three men in charge of the contractor involved in the Wang Fuk Court renovation project.
The same three were previously detained by police on suspicion of manslaughter and released on Friday night, before being arrested again by the commission as part of the corruption probe.
National security police arrested a man on suspicion of intent to incite sedition by taking advantage of the fire. The Post earlier learned that a founder named “Miles” of a petition for the government to address “four demands” on follow-up work to deal with the fire had been taken in for questioning by national security police. It remains unclear whether “Miles” has been arrested.
Without naming anyone, Beijing’s Office for Safeguarding National Security warned that “anti-China and ill-intentioned individuals” were attempting to “disrupt Hong Kong by exploiting the disaster”.
An office spokesman said some malicious individuals were spreading false information, attacking the government’s rescue efforts, and inciting social division, confrontation and hatred against authorities and the chief executive amid the tragedy.
“They exploit people’s pain and go against public opinion, attempting to use the victims’ grief to advance their political ambitions, drag Hong Kong back into the turmoil of the 2019 social unrest,” he said.




Local authorities also announced the establishment of a new interdepartmental fire investigation task force to determine the cause of the blaze and how it spread, as well as to identify the factors that led to the heavy casualties.
The Buildings Department ordered work suspension at 30 private building projects, including 28 that used the same contractor as Wang Fuk Court, Prestige Construction & Engineering Company, citing its “lack of confidence” in the firm’s ability to ensure site safety.
As the city continued its probe, China’s State Council Safety Committee ordered a nationwide “investigation and rectification campaign” to eliminate major fire risks in high-rise residential and public buildings.
The Ministry of Emergency Management said that the campaign specifically targeted buildings currently undergoing external wall renovations or interior modifications – the circumstances that turned the Wang Fuk Court blaze into a catastrophe.

Key violations under immediate scrutiny included the use of flammable or combustible materials in new external insulation systems, the deployment of banned bamboo or wooden scaffolding and non-flame-retardant safety netting, as well as unauthorised construction.
Property owners and management companies must conduct immediate self-inspections, while local authorities were instructed to carry out on-site checks, demand rapid corrections and set strict deadlines for resolving entrenched problems. Serious violations would trigger harsh penalties and accountability measures, it warned.
In Tai Po, more than 600 officers from police’s Disaster Victims Identification Unit searched two blocks, Wang Yan House and Wang Tao House, where fires were first put out. Assistant police commissioner Lam Man-han said no human remains were found, while officers rescued three cats and a turtle.
She highlighted the difficulties officers faced, including the dark and narrow environment and flooding inside.
Lam said she expected the on-site investigation process to be completed within three to four weeks, pledging to reopen the place to residents as soon as possible.

Chief Superintendent Karen Tsang Shuk-yin said the fire had resulted in 128 deaths and 83 injuries as of 3pm on Saturday. She said 84 fatalities and 37 injured were confirmed as being among the missing, and 44 bodies had yet to be identified.
About 150 others remained “unreachable”, she added.
“Within these 150 cases, there are 100 cases where we have received only scant details,” she said. “We are contacting these people who have called our hotline one by one to get some more information, so we can push ahead with our investigation.”
The Indonesian consulate told the Post that seven domestic helpers from the country were confirmed to have died in the fire as of Friday evening, while another two were injured. It said 140 of its nationals lived and worked in Wang Fuk Court, and as of Saturday morning, 79 remained unaccounted for.
The Philippine consulate said a domestic helper – previously identified as missing – died in the blaze. Up to 79 Filipino domestic helpers had been confirmed as safe.
Public generosity accompanied the citywide mourning over the victims, prompting authorities to ask residents to stop donating supplies for the time being.
“The resources we have now are abundant,” Deputy Chief Secretary Warner Cheuk Wing-hing said, adding that authorities needed to avoid waste.
Reporting by Fiona Sun, Harvey Kong, Mike Chan, Edith Lin, Jess Ma and Wynna Wong.

data.title: Hong Kong’s national security police question man over fire response petition
data.description:
Hong Kong’s national security police have questioned an individual who allegedly launched a petition calling on the government to address four demands following the deadly blaze in Tai Po, the Post has learned.
The move on Saturday came as the central government’s national security authorities in Hong Kong warned against “anti-China and ill-intentioned individuals” exploiting the tragedy in an attempt to disrupt the city.
A source said the man taken to a police station for questoning was identified as “Miles”.
It was reported that a person called Miles Kwan and a group of unidentified others had set up the Tai Po Wang Fuk Court Fire Concern Group, which launched the petition calling on the government to address four demands in the aftermath of the deadly inferno at the Wang Fuk Court residential complex that killed at least 128 people.
It calls for providing support for affected residents, examining the system for supervising construction work, setting up an independent commission of inquiry and pursuing accountability while holding government officials responsible.
According to a check on Saturday evening, the group’s Instagram page and the link to participate in the petition were no longer available.
The Post has reached out to the force for comment.
The Saturday statement from the Office for Safeguarding National Security did not point to any specific body or individual, but it accused “anti-China” and “malicious individuals” of spreading false information, attacking the government’s rescue efforts, inciting social division and stirring hatred against authorities and the chief executive.
It said such acts amounted to defying humanity, and those responsible were bound to face moral condemnation and legal punishment.
“They exploit people’s pain and go against public opinion, attempting to use the victims’ grief to advance their political ambitions and drag Hong Kong back into the turmoil of the 2019 social unrest,” an office spokesman said.
“We sternly warn the anti-China disruptors who attempt to ‘disrupt Hong Kong through disaster’. No matter what methods you use, you will certainly be held accountable and strictly punished under the Hong Kong national security law and the Safeguarding National Security Ordinance.”
During the 2019 anti-government protests, demonstrators rallied behind a list of demands and chanted the slogan “five demands, not one less”.
The office spokesman also underscored its support for the Hong Kong government in cracking down on acts trying to disrupt the city during a tragedy, punishing acts endangering national security and counteracting any interference from external forces.
It stressed the government, backed by the central authorities and the motherland, led all sectors of society in the response to the disaster, while departments also promptly clarified the relevant facts.
“They exposed and refuted the evil plots of disruptors to mislead and confuse the public and investigated and curbed the unruly words and actions of those with ulterior motives who seek to exploit the disaster to create unrest and disrupt Hong Kong,” the spokesman said.
The office called on the public to remain vigilant against efforts by individuals with malicious intentions who provoked social division and confrontation under the guise of speaking for the people.
It said the public should stand together in solidarity and help the government in the aftermath of the tragedy.

data.title: 3 cats and turtle rescued from Hong Kong residential complex days after fire
data.description:
Several more pets have been rescued from the burned buildings of a residential complex in Tai Po, as animal charities continued to search for separated owners and expressed concerns that animals still trapped will have gone days without food or water.
Authorities said on Saturday that a sweep of two blocks where flames were first extinguished at Wang Fuk Court – Wang Yan House and Wang Tao House – found no human remains, but three cats and a turtle were rescued and passed to the Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals (SPCA).
The city’s largest animal charity said on Friday afternoon that 243 animals had been recorded as “affected” by the fire, with 92 found as of that morning. It did not have a breakdown on how many were alive. The rest remained unaccounted for.
“At this stage, our greatest concern is that the pets at Wang Chi [House] have been without food and water for three days and nights,” the SPCA said on Saturday, referring to the only block out of the estate’s eight buildings that was unaffected by the inferno.

But residents have been unable to return home to fetch their pets given the entire complex has been blocked off, as rescuers concentrated their efforts on the other seven buildings.
The SPCA said it was currently coordinating with the Fire Services Department and police in hopes of finding a safe way to rescue the animals.
On social media, residents have called for any officers or rescue staff who may be able to enter the building to help find their pets, which included dogs and cats that needed medication.
Responding to the Post, an SPCA spokeswoman added: “For animals that have not been directly affected by the fire, it is still important that we reach them soon, as after three days, they face the risk of starvation, dehydration and missed medication for those with chronic health conditions.
“Also, fear and stress can trigger further medical issues, so the sooner we can reunite them with their owners, the better.”
The SPCA has called on Wang Chi House pet owners to call their hotline at 9503 6229 to make themselves known, while other Wang Fuk Court owners that had yet to register with the society should call 6040 6624.
The spokeswoman said animals that had been exposed to smoke or heat could be suffering from severe respiratory issues, burns or wounds that could require immediate attention, and it was vital for them to receive care as soon as possible.
As for rescued pets, she called on caretakers to pay close attention to symptoms that could make a late appearance.
“Even if pets appear fine, smoke inhalation and toxic gas exposure can cause delayed symptoms, sometimes only showing up 24 to 48 hours [later] or even several days after the fire,” she said.
“Burns and skin lesions might not be immediately apparent under the fur.”
Common signs to watch for included coughing, fast and shallow or laboured breathing, or panting – open mouth breathing – in cats.
Other symptoms to watch for included red eyes, burns or soot on the face, skin redness, scabbing, sudden hair loss, lethargy, weakness or fainting.
“We recommend that all rescued pets be seen by a veterinarian as soon as possible, even if they seem healthy,” she said.
Along with the SPCA, a number of animal charities have been regularly posting online in an attempt to connect owners with their lost pets.

data.title: Sense of sombre unity as Hong Kong mourns 128 killed in blaze
data.description:
A sense of sombre unity permeated the whole of Hong Kong on Saturday as residents from all walks of life mourned the victims of the city’s deadliest fire in seven decades by signing condolence books or flooding areas near the scene of the inferno with bouquets.
By Saturday evening, lengthy queues had grown near ground zero, Wang Fuk Court in Tai Po, with residents of all ages silently lining up with flowers in hand, turning several memorial sites into a sea of bouquets to remember those killed in the blaze that erupted on Wednesday.
At least 128 people were killed and 83 injured by the fire that engulfed seven of the eight 31-storey residential blocks.
It took Eddy Au Yeung, a 41-year-old who works in the import and export sector, at least 45 minutes for his turn.
“I live in Hong Kong. This estate and its many blocks have a lot of residents who lost their homes and their families; it is something that is very hard to accept,” he said.
“When I saw the site with my eyes for the first time, I thought that it was in such a horrible place, it was almost like hell on Earth. Being inside must have been very hard.”


Flowers were also piling up outside Sha Tin Fire Station as people paid tribute to fallen 37-year-old firefighter Ho Wai-ho, who served there before he was killed on Wednesday.
During the day, residents streamed into the condolence points set up across the 18 districts after the doors opened at 9am.
Among those who arrived at Fu Heng Neighbourhood Community Centre in Tai Po were Man Wong, who barely held back his tears when he talked about the victims, and his pregnant wife.
“I came here just to mourn the lost lives, we are Tai Po people after all, and we all lived around here,” he said. “I hoped those who passed away rest in peace, and those who survived could get through their pain quickly.”




Wong, a resident of the nearby Sun Hing Garden, added that he was surprised by the rate at which the fire had spread.
“I first saw the clips about the fire on Facebook; it started from the ground floor of the first building and burned upwards. It was all very dramatic,” the 39-year-old said.
“Our estate also had its major maintenance done. It’s about to finish. If it had happened to us, I’d find it to be a terrifying thing.”
He called on the government to strengthen inspection and maintenance efforts while playing its gatekeeping role well to avoid a repeat of such tragedy.
A woman, who had lived in the neighbourhood for decades and wished to be identified as Mrs Wong, also came to offer her condolences.
“I lost people I knew in the fire – he’s the father of my son’s classmate,” she said. “I know I can’t do much, but I hope those who can will donate to the residents affected by the fire hazard.
“I’m speechless. I just hope they can pull it through this difficult time.”

Residents also flocked to Sai Ying Pun Community Hall on Hong Kong Island to express their sorrow for the victims.
Caleb Leung, who works in the finance sector and has lived in Tai Po for five years, had empathy for those living in the neighbourhood.
“For incidents like this, everyone is going to be affected. Everybody knows everybody there. It has hit the community pretty hard,” he said.
Leung said he expected that the fire would prompt tighter future oversight of construction work.
A 30-year-old woman who would only give her name as Lucy advocated stronger citywide safety measures and mental health education.
“Apart from signing [condolence books], what we should really do is focus on making the city a safer place, what caused the fire to happen and how to regulate it and also maybe providing more mental health lectures and resources,” she said.
“We need more than just telling citizens it’s OK to feel sad.”

data.title: Israeli drone attack kills 2 Gaza children, latest victims of violence despite ceasefire
data.description:
An Israeli attack killed two children in Gaza on Saturday, medics and relatives said, in violence that has persisted in the Palestinian enclave despite a fragile ceasefire.
The children’s uncle said an Israeli drone fired on Fadi and Goma Abu Assi, brothers aged 10 and 12, while they were gathering firewood to help their wheelchair-bound father east of Khan Younis in southern Gaza.
“They are children...what did they do? They do not have missiles or bombs, they went to gather wood for their father so he can start a fire,” Mohamed Abu Assi told Reuters as their funeral took place.
At the funeral, the children’s father wept over the body of one of the boys whose white shroud had been peeled back to show his face.
The Israeli military did not immediately respond to a Reuters request for comment.
At least 354 Palestinians have been killed since the ceasefire between Israel and Hamas went into effect on October 10, Gazan health authorities say. Israel says three of its soldiers have been killed by militants’ gunfire in that time.


The level of violence has reduced since the ceasefire, but Israel has continued to strike Gaza and conduct demolitions in territory it occupies there.
Palestinian militant group Hamas and Israel signed a truce on October 9 after two years of war but left the most intractable disputes for further talks.
Israel and Hamas have accused each other of violating the ceasefire commitments and of pushing back against later steps required by US President Donald Trump’s 20-point plan.
Israel’s offensive in Gaza has killed more than 69,700 Palestinians, most of them civilians, health officials in Gaza say. Much of the territory is in ruins.
Israel began its offensive after a Hamas-led attack on Israel on October 7, 2023, in which 1,200 people were killed and about 250 were taken to Gaza as hostages, according to Israel’s tally.

data.title: Venezuela airspace considered ‘closed in its entirety’, says Trump
data.description:
US President Donald Trump warned on Saturday that the airspace above and near Venezuela should be considered closed, the latest escalation in a stand-off with leftist leader Nicolas Maduro.
“To all Airlines, Pilots, Drug Dealers, and Human Traffickers,” Trump wrote on his Truth Social network, “please consider THE AIRSPACE ABOVE AND SURROUNDING VENEZUELA TO BE CLOSED IN ITS ENTIRETY”.
The president did not elaborate.
Trump’s administration is piling pressure on Venezuela, with a major military deployment in the Caribbean that includes the world’s largest aircraft carrier.
Washington says the aim is to curb drug trafficking, but Caracas insists regime change is the ultimate goal.
US forces have carried out strikes against more than 20 alleged Venezuelan drug-smuggling vessels in the Caribbean Sea and eastern Pacific Ocean since early September, killing over 80 people.
Washington has yet to release evidence that the vessels it targeted were used to smuggle drugs or posed a threat to the United States.

Regional tensions have flared as a result of the campaign and the accompanying military build-up.
US aviation authorities last week urged civilian aircraft operating in Venezuelan airspace to “exercise caution” due to the “worsening security situation and heightened military activity in or around Venezuela”.
That warning prompted six airlines that account for much of the travel in South America to suspend flights to Venezuela.
The move infuriated Caracas and led it to ban the companies - Spain’s Iberia, Portugal’s TAP, Colombia’s Avianca, Chile and Brazil’s LATAM, Brazil’s GOL and Turkish Airlines -- for “joining the actions of state terrorism promoted by the United States government”.
The New York Times reported on Friday that Trump and Maduro had spoken by phone last week and discussed a possible meeting in the United States.
The report about the Trump-Maduro call came a day after the US president said efforts to halt Venezuelan drug trafficking by land were imminent, further ratcheting up tensions with Caracas.

data.title: China takes on US and Russia as it touts J-35 fighter jet in the Middle East
data.description:
China’s second fifth-generation fighter jet, the J-35, could find buyers in the Middle East as demand grows for advanced stealth aircraft in the region – though its capabilities remain unknown, according to analysts.
China, the United States and Russia are the only countries making fifth-generation fighter jets, and all three showcased their aircraft at last week’s Dubai Air Show.
A model of the J-35 – made by China National Aero-Technology Import & Export Corporation (Catic) and with a design similar to that of the American F-35 – was on display at the event.
Fifth-generation fighters typically feature stealth and supercruise capabilities – meaning they can cruise at supersonic speed without using their afterburners. They are also equipped with integrated avionics, internal weapons bays and advanced sensors.
In addition, their high manoeuvrability is combined with enhanced situational awareness, making them more effective in air-to-air and air-to-ground roles than older aircraft.


The J-35 was designed as a fighter jet for the Fujian aircraft carrier, which recently entered service, and was first made by Shenyang Aircraft Corporation. The model on show in Dubai was a land-based variant, the J-35A.
Unlike the J-20 – China’s first fifth-generation fighter jet, which is subject to an export ban – the J-35 has been offered to overseas buyers at trade shows in recent years.
It was also featured at the Paris Air Show in June and at the International Defence Exhibition and Conference in Abu Dhabi in February, where it made its debut outside China after being unveiled in Zhuhai in November last year.
Gareth Jennings, aviation editor at global military intelligence company Janes, said the J-35 could serve clients who were price sensitive and excluded from importing Western systems like the F-35 but did not want to align too closely with Russia.
He said potential buyers included those seeking fast delivery times and fewer political constraints on their purchases – factors that were “more pertinent” to the Middle East market.
“Its chief obstacle to export success is that it remains an unknown quantity in terms of capabilities, build quality, reliability, et cetera – true of Chinese military products in general, rather than just the J-35,” Jennings said.
“However, the recent reported good showing of Chinese-built products in the recent Indian-Pakistan clashes … will have certainly allayed most of those concerns.”
He was referring to Pakistan’s Chinese-made 4.5-generation J-10C fighter jet that shot down at least one of India’s French-made Rafale fighter jets near the disputed Kashmir region in May.

David Roberts, a reader in international security and Middle East studies at King’s College London, said the Gulf monarchies would be keen to acquire high-end technology from China given its already “small but significant” sales of ballistic missiles and armed drones to Saudi Arabia, Qatar and the United Arab Emirates.
But he said there were still issues “preventing or at least hampering” China from selling weapons systems such as the J-35.
“These are complex machines that require a vast and decades-long training, logistical and procurement package – you can’t just plug and play them, switching as geopolitical whims change,” Roberts said.
He added that “China would not want one of its aircraft consistently flying around a region teeming with Western radar equipment”.
China is touting its J-35 jets in the Middle East at a time when there is growing demand for air superiority capabilities as tensions in the region escalate, including over Gaza where a fragile ceasefire is in place.
The UAE and Saudi Arabia have been actively looking to procure advanced fighters for their air forces, driving competition for defence exports to the region.
American defence contractor Lockheed Martin’s F-35 and Russia’s Su-57, manufactured by Sukhoi, were both on show at the event in Dubai last week.
Their appearance at the air display was followed by President Donald Trump’s announcement that he had approved the sale of F-35s to “great ally” Saudi Arabia, a day before Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman’s visit to the US.
The planned sale marks a departure from Washington’s previous limits on selling advanced stealth fighter jets to Middle Eastern countries.
However, it could still be rejected by a congressional review given that US law mandates Israel to maintain a qualitative military edge over its regional adversaries, and with close ties between China and Arab countries raising concerns in Washington over a potential technological security breach.
It comes as Riyadh continues to refuse to establish official ties with Tel Aviv under the Abraham Accords – a series of agreements brokered by Washington that has led to the normalisation of diplomatic relations between Israel and several Arab nations.
The F-35 is widely regarded as the world’s most advanced fighter jet. It has been exported to 12 countries and several others are awaiting delivery – all of them US allies. Israel currently operates 46 of the warplanes.
During his first term, Trump approved the sale of F-35 jets to the UAE after Abu Dhabi agreed to establish formal ties with Israel in 2020.
But the deal fell apart after Joe Biden took office in 2021, citing increasing military cooperation between the UAE and China that could risk a technology security breach involving one of America’s most advanced fighter jets.
According to Jennings, the sale of F-35s to Saudi Arabia, the UAE or anywhere else in the Middle East would depend on whether Israel could maintain an edge in the region on its military capabilities.
“It is worth noting that it was the UAE’s deepening ties with China and the risks this posed to F-35 security that eventually scuppered that deal,” he said.
“With Israel enjoying deepening relations with Saudi Arabia … its attitude towards a potential F-35 sale could be tied in with developing closer bilateral relations between the two countries.”




Liselotte Odgaard, a senior fellow at the Hudson Institute in Washington, said selling F-35s to important strategic partners such as the UAE and Saudi Arabia would be both “profitable for the US and also a means of establishing technological and logistical dependency” on the US and its allies.
“The administration may prioritise major arms deals as tools of diplomatic alignment and economic profit, and it would be in line with previous attempts at the UAE deal under the Abraham Accords framework. How the push for a Saudi deal plays out may be a good indicator,” she said.
“The most significant hurdle in the US Congress is the Saudi security and defence ties with China that put sensitive F-35 technology at risk if the deal goes through. Any deal would face significant congressional scrutiny for this reason.”
In contrast to the F-35 exports, Russia’s Su-57 has reportedly secured only one overseas buyer, with Algeria reportedly ordering eight of the fighter jets.
First flown in 2010 and introduced to the Russian military in 2020, the Su-57 is the country’s first fighter jet with full stealth capability and has reportedly been used in Russia’s war in Ukraine.
Its export variant, the Su-57E, was unveiled at the air show in Zhuhai last year.
But international sanctions on Russia following its invasion of Ukraine have brought supply chain constraints for its aerospace and defence industries, raising concerns over whether it can deliver the aircraft on schedule as well as provide maintenance, repair and overhaul in the future.
Roberts from King’s College London said the Gulf monarchies tended to find ways to demonstrate their “independence”, to show they were “not in the US’ pocket” – and inviting Russia to the Dubai Air Show was an “obvious example”.
“Pragmatically, they may also wish to encourage Russian engagement in the UAE too – some kind of a technology sharing approach has long been broadly mooted,” he said.
“It remains unlikely that they will acquire the Su-57, but doesn’t hurt to keep others – US, UK, France – on their toes.”
Odgaard said sanctions against Russia called into question whether it could provide reliable long-term upgrades and logistics support for the Su-57 and that the pace of production had been “slow and inconsistent for the past couple of years”.
She also noted that the Russian aircraft was not at the same level as the F-35 on stealth capabilities and avionics.

data.title: Hong Kong minister urges NGOs to work more closely with government over fire aid
data.description:
Hong Kong’s home affairs minister has called on NGOs to better coordinate with the government on their support for residents displaced in the city’s deadliest fire in decades following reported confusion on the ground.
Secretary for Home and Youth Affairs Alice Mak Mei-kuen on Saturday also rejected accusations the government-backed care teams had called police to disperse volunteers manning makeshift stations offering supplies to affected residents near the scene of the fire at Wang Fuk Court in Tai Po.
“Many charitable organisations and NGOs want to offer help immediately. They rush to the temporary shelters and set up counters, start distributing subsidies or relief supplies. This makes the affected residents queue up at multiple counters,” she said, addressing complaints from affected residents on the chaos and long queues they had to endure in seeking assistance.

While expressing gratitude to the NGOs, Mak urged them to liaise with the Social Welfare Department to improve coordination.
“The best approach is for all organisations to contact the Social Welfare Department and keep the residents informed about the available subsidies, allowing the department staff to assist residents with the applications all at once, without having to queue multiple times,” she said.
Deputy Chief Secretary Warner Cheuk Wing-hing also appealed to members of the public to halt donations for now to avoid wastage given resources were now sufficient.
He said the government would set up a manned central supply station in the Tai Po Community Centre to manage the collected resources and allocate them.
Donors should first register their information and the supplies they have through WhatsApp number 9213 2388, he said.
After large quantities of donated supplies continuously poured following Wednesday’s tragedy, the makeshift supply stations at an open plaza next to Wang Fuk Court were vacated on Saturday morning.
One volunteer, who declined to give her name, said she had received a message to clear the area in the early hours of Saturday. She said that instructions came from a “government department” via police, with no reason given, but the Post could not verify her claim.
“We plan first to find a warehouse to store the supplies and then give them to different social welfare organisations,” she said. “We don’t know the location yet, as we have not been able to reach groups as it is Saturday.
Hundreds of volunteers have been gathering in the vicinity of Wang Fuk Court since Thursday, running the makeshift booths offering affected residents supplies ranging from food, clothes to pet food, in a bottom-up effort.
Tensions between the government-backed care teams and volunteers had mounted in recent days, with complaints posted online accusing the care teams of taking group photos instead of focusing on the work at hand.
Mak dismissed suggestions the teams had asked police to disperse the volunteers, as well as rumours the members had been rude to residents.
She stressed authorities were responsible for maintaining order in the shelters and protecting residents’ privacy and safety.
“The residents have already suffered a huge shock, and ... we have a responsibility to protect their safety and privacy, and ensure the order, safety and hygiene in the temporary shelters,” she said.
Hundreds of volunteers have been gathering in the vicinity of Wang Fuk Court since Thursday, running the makeshift booths offering affected residents supplies ranging from food and clothes to pet food.

data.title: Spain stops pork exports to China after African swine flu cases, first in 30 years
data.description:
Spain’s Agriculture Ministry said it has halted all pork exports to China after two wild boar found dead near Barcelona tested positive for African swine flu, the first cases in three decades.
The outbreak, the first since 1994, coincides with Spanish efforts to court Beijing and gain market share in the pork sector and after China imposed tariffs on EU pork during an anti-dumping investigation.
Spain is the European Union’s leading pork producer, accounting for roughly a quarter of the bloc’s output, ahead of Germany, with annual pork exports of about 3.5 billion euros (US$4.05 billion).
The Director-General of Agri-Food Production Health and Animal Welfare, Emilio Garcia, told reporters the measure was implemented in line with a recently agreed bilateral protocol with Beijing.
Once China declares that protocol has been activated it will be able to resume exports from regions that are not affected by the virus.

Barcelona is in the Catalonia region, which accounts for about seven per cent of the country’s pork farms, according to Agriculture Ministry figures.
“It’s not good news. The European market is already struggling after a 20 per cent drop in prices since July,” said Jean-Paul Simier, a meat analyst at French commodities research group Cyclope.
“There is a risk of an embargo against the biggest EU pork exporter, notably in Asia, and China in particular.”
Spanish pork lobby Interporc said pork farms within a 20km (12.4-mile) radius of the location where the dead animals were found would face restrictions on operating and selling their products.
The recently signed agreement will mean the rest of Spain’s pork producers would be able to continue to export to China and the EU, although other countries such as Japan could impose blanket bans, said Daniel de Miguel, Interporc´s international director.


There are no restrictions on pork exports to the EU outside the 20-kilometre radius of a wooded area near Barcelona, where five pig farms operate, García Muro said.
Spain’s farmers’ association Asaja said the sector was ready to face the outbreak, but said authorities needed to address an “out of control presence” of wild animals such as boars and rabbits in rural areas that risked contaminating livestock.
“We have spent years modernising farms, reinforcing biosecurity and making our operations among the most advanced in the world,” it said.
The ministry said it had notified the European Union and activated emergency measures in the affected area, urging pig farms to tighten security measures while investigators try to find the source of the infection.
The virus, which is harmless to humans but deadly for pigs, has been spreading westwards in Europe in recent years.
Germany’s sizeable pork industry has already been rattled, with many overseas countries imposing bans on its pork. In recent months Croatia has been trying to contain an outbreak.
